unresolved.text: If *P. carinii* pneumonia is the correct diagnosis, the diagnostic procedures used should have revealed the organism, given their high sensitivity.
If the patient had pneumocystis pneumonia, the response to conventional antibiotic therapy should have been prompt.
If the patient had a bacterial infection, the clinical presentation and response to treatment should have been consistent with that diagnosis.

I think that the most likely diagnosis in this case is a primary amebic liver abscess, although I am troubled by the negative serologic test.

The coincidence of sinus pain and lung nodules raises the possibility of Wegener's granulomatosis is not mentioned, however, several features of this patient’s presentation are suggestive of Crohn’s disease, which is relatively common, with an incidence of approximately 10 cases per 100,000 persons is not mentioned, instead, Did this patient have a community-acquired pneumonia?

Although *P. carinii* is the most common single pathogen causing pneumonia in patients with AIDS, occurring in 80 percent of such patients during the course of their illness, the failure to recover the organisms by either induction of sputum or bronchoalveolar lavage and transbronchial biopsy, the progression of the interstitial infiltrates over a period of several months despite prophylaxis with aerosolized pentamidine, and finally the appearance of hilar lymphadenopathy are against a diagnosis of pneumocystis pneumonia.

The coincidence of the patient's ethnic background, the tempo of his disease, and the radiographic pattern of diffuse interstitial pulmonary infiltrates are all consistent with pneumonia caused by *P. carinii*.
If this patient had pneumocystis pneumonia, we would expect to see crackles and wheezing on examination of the chest, but they are rarely heard in patients with pneumocystis pneumonia.
The many types of proctitis can be grouped on the basis of underlying cause, such as infection, ulceration, proctocolitis, autoimmune disease, trauma, cancer, ischemia, and amyloidosis.

The problem with the diagnosis of a primary hepatic malignant tumor is the presentation, and we must first rule out other diagnoses, such as a secondary infection of a hydatid cyst, a pyogenic liver abscess, or an infected hepatic tumor.
The histopathological features, the distribution of the affected arteries, and the absence of antibodies specific to myeloperoxidase or proteinase 3 rule out an ANCA-related vasculitis.
The pattern of diffuse interstitial pulmonary infiltrates with possible hilar lymphadenopathy is typical of an amebic abscess and is present in the great majority of the cases.
I think that the most likely diagnosis in this case is a primary amebic liver abscess, although I am troubled by the negative serologic test.
Although most cases of giant-cell arteritis involve the temporal artery, involvement of the larger arteries has been reported in about 10 percent of patients.

The pattern matches the description by Wilkinson and Russell in their classic review of giant-cell arteritis.
The finding of a large, solitary mass within the right lobe of the liver is typical of an amebic abscess and is present in the great majority of the cases.
I think that the most likely diagnosis in this case is a primary amebic liver abscess.

Since I cannot exclude the alternative diagnoses, such as a secondary infection of a hydatid cyst, a pyogenic liver abscess, or an infected hepatic tumor, I think that the surgeon should have proceeded with all these diagnoses in mind.
